headline: Taxi Industry Collapse Leaves 48,000 Drivers Without Income
context: Malaysia's traditional taxi industry reported that 48,000 of its 78,000 licensed drivers earned below RM1,500 monthly in 2025, with e-hailing services capturing 82% of urban point-to-point transport market.

cards.big: 48,000 taxi drivers earn below RM1,500 monthly — e-hailing captured 82% of their market in under 8 years
cards.sub: Average taxi driver age is 54 — too old for most alternative employment and too young to retire

cards.big: Taxi medallion values collapsed from RM120,000 to RM18,000 — destroying lifetime savings of drivers who invested in permits
cards.sub: E-hailing companies pay zero medallion equivalent while operating in the same market — regulatory asymmetry destroyed taxi asset values

cards.big: 78% of taxi drivers are Malay males over 50 — a demographic with the fewest alternative employment options
cards.sub: Transition to e-hailing requires smartphone proficiency, newer car, and working capital — barriers most veteran taxi drivers can't clear

cards.big: SPAD was abolished in 2018 and replaced by APAD — regulatory confusion during transition left taxi industry without advocate
cards.sub: No compensation scheme for permit value destruction — government allowed creative destruction without destruction compensation

cards.big: Welcoming e-hailing was right — abandoning 48,000 taxi drivers without transition support was an act of policy negligence
Should the government offer RM50,000 transition grants for taxi drivers to retrain or purchase e-hailing compliant vehicles?
